title: macOS 27 Golden Gate Guide: All the new features coming to compatible Macs, 2026 release date and more
content:
Macworld


Apple has officially unveiled macOS 27 Golden Gate, the next major update for the Mac, bringing Apple’s next-generation Apple Intelligence platform to compatible devices. The update introduces an all-new Siri AI assistant, major upgrades to apps including Safari, Photos, Messages, Mail and Calendar, as well as expanded child safety features, design refinements and performance improvements throughout the operating system.
Golden Gate continues Apple’s tradition of naming macOS releases after iconic Californian landmarks. Among the headline additions are Siri AI, a completely redesigned version of Siri powered by Apple Intelligence, new AI-powered tools across Apple’s apps, enhanced photo editing capabilities, smarter web browsing in Safari, and new automation features that make it easier to get things done on a Mac.
macOS 27 will be supported on all Macs powered by Apple silicon, marking the end of the road for Intel-based Macs. While Intel models are no longer eligible for the new operating system, Apple is expected to continue providing security updates for those devices for some time.
The developer and public betas of macOS 27 are available now. Following several months of testing, Apple is expected to release the finished version to all users this autumn. This hub covers everything Apple has announced for macOS 27 Golden Gate, including new features, compatibility details, beta release information, Apple Intelligence enhancements and the all-new Siri AI experience.


What’s New in macOS 27 Golden Gate at a Glance

				
					

				
			
Apple





Siri AI: A completely redesigned Siri powered by Apple Intelligence that can understand personal context, answer questions from the web, and take actions across apps.
Visual Intelligence on Mac: Ask questions about anything on your screen, including images, files and text, using a dedicated shortcut.
Safari AI Tools: Automatically groups related tabs, monitors websites for changes such as price drops, and can even generate custom extensions from a description.
Apple Intelligence across apps: Adds AI-powered features to Messages, Mail, Calendar, Photos and Shortcuts to help write, organise, edit and automate tasks.
Child Safety Upgrades: Introduces Ask to Browse, Time Allowances, enhanced Communication Safety and redesigned parental controls to help keep children safe online. 






Siri AI: Siri powered macOS 27 features  

				
					

				
			
Foiundry




Apple’s biggest focus in macOS 27 Golden Gate is Siri AI, a completely redesigned version of Siri powered by Apple Intelligence. Rather than functioning as a simple voice assistant, Siri AI is designed to act as a conversational AI assistant that can answer questions, understand personal information, access web knowledge, and perform actions across apps and services.
At a glance: What’s new with Siri AI?
Conversational AI assistant: Siri AI will be far more capable than previous versions of Siri, according to Apple, allowing users to ask open-ended questions, brainstorm ideas, conduct research, and hold natural back-and-forth conversations rather than relying on simple voice commands.
Dedicated Siri app: macOS 27 will offer a standalone Siri app that stores conversations in one place and sync them across Apple devices through iCloud. For example, users could start a conversation on their Mac and continue it later on their iPhone or iPad.
Personal context awareness: Siri will be able to understand information stored across your messages, emails, notes, photos and files. Apple says Siri will be able to locate specific information, find content you’ve saved in the past, and surface relevant details based on your personal data.
Web-powered answers: Unlike previous versions of Siri, Siri AI will be able to access up-to-date information from the web to answer questions on virtually any topic. Apple says it will be able to help with research, interview preparation, learning new subjects and gathering information quickly.
App actions and automation: Siri AI will be able to take action across apps including Messages, Music, Reminders and more. Users will be able to edit messages, manage playlists, create reminders and perform other tasks using natural language rather than navigating menus manually.
Spotlight integration: Siri AI will be built directly into Spotlight Search on macOS. Users will be able to type questions into Spotlight, receive AI-generated answers, and launch Siri-powered searches without opening a separate app or switching to a web browser.
Visual Intelligence on Mac: For the first time, Visual Intelligence is coming to macOS. Users will be able to select content on screen and ask Siri questions about images, files, documents and text, allowing the assistant to understand and respond to onscreen content.
Integrated Writing Tools: Siri AI will generate text from scratch, rewrite drafts, summarise content and refine existing writing. Apple says it will even adapt its tone, wording and punctuation to match how users typically communicate with individual contacts.
Improved dictation: Apple says it will significantly upgrade systemwide dictation with better speech recognition. Dictation will automatically add punctuation, capitalisation and formatting while improving accuracy, making it easier to turn spoken words into polished text.
Custom Siri voices: Users will be able to personalise Siri by selecting a voice and adjusting its pace and expressiveness. Apple says this allows Siri to sound more natural and better suited to individual preferences, although the feature requires newer Apple silicon hardware.
Availability: Apple says Siri AI will enter beta later this year and initially support English. It requires Apple Intelligence-compatible hardware.






Apple Intelligence across macOS 27
As well as powering Siri, the enhanced Apple Intelligence will power some other systemwide features in macOS 27. 
Systemwide Proofreading: Apple Intelligence will provide automatic proofreading and improved spelling and grammar suggestions throughout the operating system.
Intelligent File and Folder Naming: In macOS 27 names for files and folders will be automatically suggested based on their contents.
Privacy: Apple emphasised that the new AI architecture maintains privacy protections. Apple claims Apple Intelligence continues to use on-device processing whenever possible and relies on Private Cloud Compute when additional processing power is required. Personal data processed through Private Cloud Compute is not stored or made accessible to Apple, and the system remains open to independent verification by security researchers.






Safari features coming in macOS 27

				
					

				
			
Notify Me in Safari

Apple




Safari is set to gain several Apple Intelligence-powered features in macOS 27, including the ability to automatically organise tabs into topics, monitor webpages for changes such as price drops and restocks with Notify Me, and generate custom browser extensions from a text description. 
Automatic Tab Topics: Safari will be able to automatically group related tabs into topics. Apple says that by continually organizing tabs as users browse, Safari will help keep research and projects organized.
Notify Me: Safari will be able to monitor web pages for changes. For example, users will be able to ask Safari to watch for product restocks, price changes, or other updates and receive notifications when those changes occur.
Describe an Extension: Users will be able to create custom Safari extensions simply by describing what they want.
Apple says Safari can generate the extension directly from a text description and add it to the toolbar.






Messages and Mail features coming in macOS 27
Messages and Mail are to gain deeper Apple Intelligence integration in macOS 27. For example, Mail and Messages are will offer smarter Smart Reply suggestions that can reflect a user’s personal writing style. 
One-Tap Suggestions in Messages: Messages will suggest actions based on conversation context. Apple says users will be able to quickly create reminders, notes, or perform other tasks directly from a conversation.
Smarter Photo Suggestions: Messages will identify and surface relevant photos based on keywords, people, and locations mentioned in chats.
Personalized Smart Reply: Both Messages and Mail will be able to mimic a user’s writing style depending on the recipient for a ‘Smart Reply’.
More Relevant Search in Mail: Mail will introduce a new search ranking system designed to surface the most relevant results first.






Photos features coming in macOS 27
A new suite of AI-powered editing tools are coming to Photos in macOS 27 that should make it easier to transform and improve photos without needing advanced editing skills.
Spatial Reframing: It will be possible to intelligently reframe images, recomposing images after they’ve been taken. 
Extend Tool: A new Extend tool will expand photos beyond their original boundaries.
Enhanced Clean Up: The updated Clean Up tool will be able to remove larger unwanted objects from images more effectively than it can presently.






Design and Interface Changes in macOS 27

				
					

				
			
Apple




macOS Golden Gate will feature several UI tweaks that address some of the criticisms of macOS 26’s Liquid Glass. For example, macOS 27 will offer improved readability and stronger contrast than Liquid Glass does currently. 
Changes include:
Improved readability with more refined Liquid Glass effects throughout macOS.
Better contrast to make interface elements easier to distinguish.
More consistent refraction effects across windows, menus and controls.
Updated toolbars with a cleaner, more unified design.
Edge-to-edge sidebars that extend fully to the edge of windows rather than appearing to float.
Refreshed menu bar icons with a more polished appearance.
Background bars behind window controls to make icon clusters stand out more clearly.
More consistent rounded window corners throughout the operating system.
Liquid Glass app icons that add greater depth and a more three-dimensional look.
Customisable Liquid Glass appearance, allowing users to adjust the effect from ultra-clear to fully tinted






Performance Improvements in macOS 27 
Apple says macOS 27 delivers performance improvements throughout the operating system, including:
Faster AirDrop transfers
Faster network file browsing
Faster Safari start page loading
macOS 27 will also add support for higher-resolution ultrawide displays, including 5K at 120Hz. Display arrangements will also be remembered more reliably when reconnecting monitors.






macOS 27 Compatible Macs 

				
					

				
			
The MacBook Neo and other Macs in Apple’s current lineup will all be compatible with macOS 27.

Foundry




Apple confirmed that macOS 27 Golden Gate is compatible with:
MacBook Neo (A18 Pro, 2026)
MacBook Air (Apple silicon, 2020 and later)
MacBook Pro (Apple silicon, 2020 and later)
iMac (Apple silicon, 2021 and later)
Mac mini (Apple silicon, 2020 and later)
Mac Studio (2022 and later)
Mac Pro (Apple silicon, 2023 and later)
All those Macs will be able to support Apple Intelligence, but some Siri AI features require M3 with at least 12GB of unified memory or later.






macOS 27 release date: When will macOS 27 be available?
First developer beta: June 8, 2026
First public beta: July 13 2026
Final version: September 2026
Apple debut macOS 27 in a keynote at WWDC26. The official version of macOS 27 will not be available until later in 2026. In recent years, Apple has released macOS at the same time as iOS. The iOS release has been happening during the second week of September, so Monday, September 14, is our guess as to when macOS 27 becomes available to everyone.
When will the macOS 27 beta be released?

				
					

				
			

Apple




However, it isn’t necessary to wait until the fall to try out the new features in macOS 27. Apple released the first developer beta at WWDC. The first public beta became available on July 13, 2026.
The beta cycle includes several iterations as Apple refines the software. Here’s how you can participate in the beta program.
We have an Apple WWDC event blog if you want to read expert commentary and our immediate reactions and what the WWDC news means to you.






 FAQ 


1.
When is macOS 27 coming out
Apple hasn’t confirmed exactly when macOS 27 will be available to install, but it usually launches to the general public in September, or at the latest October. 
Once macOS 27 launches Apple will likely continue beta development to test new features that weren’t available at launch. 





2.
Should I install the macOS 27 beta?
Whether you should install the macOS 27 beta depends on your needs, technical expertise, and tolerance for bugs and instability. Beta software can introduce crashes, data loss, app compatibility problems, excessive battery drain, reduced performance, and other unexpected issues.
For most users, the recommendation is simple: don’t install it. If you’re eager to try the new features, it’s better to wait for the Public Beta and avoid installing it on your primary Mac.
Developers are a different case. The Developer Beta is available now through Apple’s Developer Program, allowing app creators to test their software against new APIs, frameworks, and system features before the public release. If you’re not a developer, think carefully before installing the Developer Beta, as it is typically less stable and more prone to bugs than later beta releases.
Regardless of which beta you choose, make sure you have a complete backup of your Mac before upgrading.





3.
Which Macs get macOS 27 
All M-series Macs will get macOS 27, and the MacBook Neo. 
Certain Siri features will only be available for M3 Macs and newer. 





4.
Should Intel Mac owners upgrade now?
Intel Mac owners should upgrade to Apple Silicon as soon as possible. The transition away from Intel hardware has reached a critical turning point, with software support rapidly vanishing and a new generation of affordable entry-level hardware making the switch more accessible than ever.
Apple has confirmed that macOS 27 will not support any Intel-based Macs. While a small number of Intel models may receive security patches for older versions of macOS until September 2028, they are already excluded from modern flagship features.
Those with Intel Macs also miss out on many features, including Apple Intelligence.





5.
What does the end of Rosetta support mean?
macOS 27 will end support for Rosetta 2, Apple’s translation technology that allows Intel-based Mac apps to run on Apple Silicon Macs.
The end of Rosetta 2 support marks the final stage of Apple’s transition away from Intel-based architecture, effectively terminating the ability of M-series Macs to run older software designed for Intel processors. Rosetta 2 has served as a “translator” or “transition layer” since 2020, allowing Apple silicon to execute x86_64 instructions.
The move further accelerates Apple’s transition away from Intel-era software. As of the macOS 26.4 update, users are already seeing Rosetta 2 warnings.





6.
When is macOS usually updated?
Here is a history of release dates for macOS:
macOS 26 Tahoe: September 15, 2025
macOS 15 Sequoia: September 16, 2024
macOS 14 Sonoma: September 26, 2023
macOS 13 Ventura: October 25, 2022
macOS 12 Monterey: October 25, 2021
macOS 11 Big Sur: November 19, 2020
macOS 10.15 Catalina: October 7, 2019
macOS 10.14 Mojave: September 24, 2018
macOS 10.13 High Sierra: September 25, 2017
macOS 10.12 Sierra: September 20, 2016

title: Apple Releases macOS Golden Gate Beta 4
content:
Apple today provided developers with the fourth beta of macOS Golden Gate, with the update coming two weeks after Apple seeded the third beta.


Developers can download ‌macOS Golden Gate‌ by going to System Settings > General > Software Update. Beta Updates will need to be enabled, and a free developer account is required.

‌macOS Golden Gate‌ includes multiple design improvements. Liquid Glass opacity has been improved to increase readability, there's a slider for controlling the level of transparency, and changes to shadowing make it easier to tell when a window is active. Apps now have uniform toolbars, edge-to-edge sidebars, and less rounded corners, plus there are fewer icons in menu bars.

Siri has been transformed into ‌Siri‌ AI, a chatbot-style assistant that combines general world knowledge, your personal data, and onscreen awareness to answer questions about anything and find information for you. ‌Siri‌ lives in Spotlight, and you can ask questions with the new Search or Ask interface.

Visual Intelligence is now on the Mac so ‌Siri‌ can answer questions about what's on your screen, and a Write with ‌Siri‌ feature lets ‌Siri‌ generate text or give feedback on your writing.

Photos app, AI tab organization in Safari, an option to use AI to automatically update weak passwords in the Passwords app, and a revamped Image Playground app that can generate photorealistic images.

found in our macOS Golden Gate roundup.

title: Apple Seeds Fourth iOS 27 and iPadOS 27 Betas to Developers
content:
Apple today seeded the fourth betas of iOS 27 and iPadOS 27 to developers for testing purposes, with the update coming two weeks after Apple released the third betas.


Registered developers can download the betas from the Settings app on the iPhone or iPad by going to the General section and selecting Software Update.

Siri, ‌Siri‌ AI. ‌Siri‌ AI is a chatbot similar to ChatGPT, incorporating general world knowledge, personal data from apps like Messages and Mail, and onscreen awareness to answer questions and find information for you.

Apple Intelligence features in apps like Photos, Safari, Shortcuts, Wallet, and Passwords, plus Apple added a standalone ‌Siri‌ app that houses ‌Siri‌ conversations. Write with ‌Siri‌ is available systemwide for generating text or offering feedback on your writing, and Visual Intelligence has been relocated to the Camera app with a new ‌Siri‌ mode.

Apple improved the look of Liquid Glass and added a slider for customizing transparency. There are multiple performance improvements to speed up iOS, so everything feels faster, even on older iPhones. Apps launch quicker, AirDrop transfers are faster, the keyboard pops up more quickly, and devices are better at transitioning between Wi-Fi and Cellular to keep you connected.

title: Apple Seeds iOS 26.6 and iPadOS 26.6 Release Candidates
content:
Apple today seeded the release candidate versions of upcoming iOS 26.6 and iPadOS 26.6 updates to developers for testing purposes, with the software coming a week after Apple seeded the fifth betas. The RCs represent the final version of the software that will be released soon should no additional bugs be found. 


Registered developers can download the betas from the Settings app on the iPhone or iPad by going to the General section and selecting Software Update.

title: diCleaner review: Powerful free Mac cleaner that needs polishing
content:
Macworld


At a glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
Clean, intuitive interface
Useful storage cleanup tools
Excellent customization options
Cons
InConsistent app uninstaller
Limited app update detection
Leftovers require manual removal
Our Verdict 
diCleaner offers an impressive collection of free Mac maintenance tools, with standout features including the excellent Space Analyzer, Large & Old Files Finder, Similar Photos Finder, and extensive customization options. However, inConsistent reliability, incomplete uninstall and leftovers removal, an ineffective App Updates tool, and bugs across several features make it feel more like an unfinished beta than a polished utility.
Price When Reviewed
This value will show the geolocated pricing text for product undefined
Best Pricing Today


				
Retailer
				
Price
				
Doinno
													
Free						
View Deal						
Product
				
Price
				

								
							
						
What is it? A free all-in-one Mac maintenance utility that combines disk cleanup, storage analysis, privacy, optimization, and application management tools.
Who is it for? Home users and small offices looking for a free Mac cleanup utility with excellent storage-analysis features and extensive customization options.
Who shouldn’t buy it? Users who need a polished, fully reliable Mac maintenance suite with dependable app uninstalling, update management, and bug-free performance.
diCleaner is a free all-in-one Mac maintenance utility from Doinno that combines disk cleanup, storage management, privacy features, startup optimisation and application management into a polished, Mac-friendly interface. Available from the Mac App Store, it requires macOS Big Sur 11.5 or later and groups its numerous tools into logical categories that make navigation straightforward.
Alongside familiar cleaning and optimisation features found in competing Mac utilities, diCleaner also introduces some original ideas, including a clipboard manager that can store up to 100 items and convenient shortcuts to macOS system settings.
Home users and small offices looking for a free utility to reclaim storage space, locate large files and tidy up their Macs will find plenty to like here. However, users who need dependable cleaning, uninstalling and maintenance tools should approach with caution, as several core features currently fail to function correctly due to software bugs that undermine what is otherwise an ambitious application.
What does diCleaner include?
diCleaner is nothing short of ambitious. Its toolset is a mix of features we’ve seen in competing applications, along with some original ideas. The interface is clean and well organized, with Cleanup, Optimize, Privacy, Applications, and Files sections, alongside quick access to Space Analyzer, Large & Old Files Finder, Duplicate Files Finder, Similar Photos Finder, Downloads Manager, Uninstall Apps, App Updates, Trash Bin, and Junk Files.

				
					

				
			
diCleaner’s Space Analyzer tool studying a user folder
.
Foundry




The Mac cleaner application also includes Startup Management, privacy tools such as Clipboard Cleanup, Remove Photo Metadata and Secure Remove, plus extensive customization options that let you fine-tune scans and create ignore lists.
Which tools are genuinely useful?
There are some handy tools to work with in diCleaner. The Startup Management tool lists login items and background items, and offers quick access to the Accessibility and Open Desktop & Dock system settings, which is a bit handier than digging through menus. The Search feature offers speedy access to macOS’ Apple Intelligence and Siri settings, as well as Notifications, which functions as a quick path and a good, original idea.
The Privacy toolset offers some interesting tools, having placed its Recent Items Cleanup, Clipboard Cleanup, Remove Photo Metadata, and Secure Remove in one section, allowing recent items to scrub documents, applications, and servers as needed. The Clipboard Cleanup works well, and the Secure Remove tool offers multiple layers of sector overwrites for added security.

				
					

				
			
diCleaner’s Secure Remove tool, complete with a multiple overwrite pass option
.
Foundry




If there was one section where diCleaner shone the brightest, it might be the Space Analyzer tool. Here, the tool offered a handy layered interface and was able to hunt down and delete a hidden 67GB virtual machine partition a copy of Parallels Desktop had left behind.
This was complemented by the Large & Old Files Finder, which offers a nice variety of search filters and can hunt for criteria such as dates, file size, and file type. The Duplicated Files Finder and Similar Photos Finder also have their strengths in that they can quickly dig through a hard drive to locate multiple copies of the same file or picture, aided by a search feature that makes the hunt that much easier. The Similar Photos Finder even offers a cool “Star Trek”-esque “Ping!” sound when it completes its task.

				
					

				
			
The Similar Photos Finder utility from the diCleaner application
.
Foundry




diCleaner is aided by an impressive amount of customization options, wherein you can customize scan rules to focus on certain file sizes as well as easily set up an Ignore List to gloss over files or applications that don’t need your attention. Poke around in the menus, and odds are you can configure diCleaner’s look and feel to something that works for you.
Which features still need work?
Still, there are some bugs to sort out. The Screenshots Manager tool only sees one screenshot in the folder; the Leftovers feature has a hard time completely removing assorted support files, and the Uninstall Apps tool was stable, but couldn’t always completely remove applications.
The App Updates tool felt extremely disappointing in that it found only updates for my App Store apps, which had already been updated and are now part of the Apple Creator Studio package, which defies the purpose of an updater tool that’s intended to scan your Mac’s applications and locate the updates you’d ordinarily forget.
Finally, the workspaces themselves need a clickable Select All box as opposed to long lists of discovered items that need to be clicked on to work with.
The end product came across as sloppy, as if the application is trying to include every macOS tool the developers could think of without taking the time to test whether it worked well or delivered what the feature set had promised.
How well does it perform?
Scans performed quickly, and this didn’t slow down my Mac, but consistent crashes with the Uninstall Apps tool were disconcerting.
Overall, performance is good when it comes to scan speed and system impact, but reliability varies between tools. Some features work exactly as intended, while others feel unfinished or inconsistent.

				
					

				
			
diCleaner’s awkward “diCleaner_MAS” file name in the macOS Finder.

Foundry




Should you use diCleaner?
It’s hard to argue with something free, but if this is meant to open the door and lead you towards the developer’s other products, diCleaner serves as a poor introduction that needed extra time for QA and debugging, especially considering the massive number of scripts and tools it’s trying to offer to the user.
The intention is there, there are some interesting tools to work with, and this would make a great utility for the home user and small office market, but an overall lack of polish among the many tools offered hampers what diCleaner could be. Still, software updates exist for a reason, and here’s hoping that Doinno can make the most of theirs and demonstrate what this application can truly be.
creator: Macworld

title: The Mac Pro could have had a very different future
content:
Macworld


The very first Macs based on Apple Silicon debuted in November 2020. However, the Mac Pro, Apple’s high-end tower Mac, didn’t get its Apple Silicon upgrade until almost three years later. When it finally made its debut at WWDC 2023, it featured an M2 Ultra chip.
Bloomberg’s Mark Gurman now reports that things could’ve been very different for the Mac Pro, in a way that would’ve left more casual Mac fans scratching their heads. According to Gurman, Apple worked on a Mac with an Intel CPU alongside the Apple Silicon model. Code-named J170, would be available to “address certain uses.”
Gurman doesn’t specify what those certain uses were, nor does he state what Intel chips Apple could’ve used. However, it wasn’t uncommon for specialized, custom software to be used on a Mac Pro–software that was written for Intel processors. While that software could’ve operated under the emulation Apple offered during the chip transition, it’s not ideal.
The main reason many Mac Pro users were resistant to Apple Silicon is the limitations it has in working with expansion cards–more specifically, third-party graphics cards. Apple Silicon has its graphics processors integrated into the chip, and discrete graphics cards couldn’t be used, which discouraged many customers with “certain uses” from the Mac Pro. So this Intel model could have solved that problem for certain demanding users.
Gurman reports that Apple also had an M3 Ultra Mac Pro in development, alongside the Intel Mac Pro. Code-named J190, it was slated to ship at the same time as the M3 Ultra Mac Studio, back in March 2025. It’s unclear why that model was never released.
Instead, Apple never updated the Mac Pro again, and eventually discontinued the line this past March. Don’t expect a Mac Pro comeback: “I don’t anticipate Apple bringing back the Mac Pro anytime soon–if ever,” said Gurman.

title: Apple ‘Extreme’ Mac chips were too good for this RAM-starved world
content:
Macworld


Take a trip back four years to June 7, 2022, when Apple announced the M2 chip at WWDC 2022. It was the base model of the M2, but reports leading up to its announcement speculated that Apple’s M2 chip plans included Max and Ultra versions, as well as a higher-end chip dubbed “Extreme.” It was thought that the M2 Extreme would find its way into the Mac Pro.
However, later in December 2022, Bloomberg’s Mark Gurman reported that Apple decided to scrap the M2 Extreme “because of both the complexity and cost of producing a processor that is essentially four M2 Max chips fused together.” Apple released the M2 Ultra the following year at WWDC 2023, and we never saw an M2 Extreme.
Fast-forward to today, where Gurman reports that Apple not only had an M2 Extreme but also an M3 Extreme, and the plans were to use those chips in the Mac Pro. But, he explains, Apple dropped the chips “due to cost and demand concerns.” You know the end of this story: Apple eventually discontinued the Mac Pro this past March.
It’s still interesting to think about what could’ve been. As you know, there’s a worldwide RAM shortage that’s only going to get worse before it gets better. Apple not only raised its Mac prices, but also memory options are limited, especially at the high-end. For example, the Mac Studio–the Mac that took the place of the Mac Pro as Apple’s highest-end model–is available only in a 96GB RAM configuration, after Apple discontinued the 256GB option due to the supply crunch. And even the 96GB is severely constrained and won’t ship for three months.
The current RAM shortage would’ve surely impacted Apple’s supply of an M3 Extreme Mac Pro. Not only would it have been extremely expensive, but between the production issues and RAM crunch, an Extreme Mac Pro would’ve ended up being a machine that everyone wanted, but no one could buy. But it’s nice to dream.

title: This MacBook Pro is cheaper than it was before Apple hiked prices
content:
Macworld


14" MacBook Pro M5 Pro 
View Deal
If you’re looking for an absolute beast of a laptop that can handle every task you need to complete, then the M5 Pro MacBook Pro is a fantastic pick, even at its new higher price point. However, over at B&H Photo it’s on sale for $2,699 with 48GB of RAM right now, so you can grab this decked-out config for $400 off. That’s $100 cheaper than it was before Apple hiked prices by $300.
This laptop is super powerful thanks to the M5 Pro chip with a 15-core CPU, a 16-core GPU, and a Neural Accelerator, making it perfect for AI tasks. You also get an insane amount of power thanks to the 48GB of unified RAM and loads of storage space in the 1TB SSD. This setup will be good to go for years to come.
The 14.2-inch Liquid Retina XDR display delivers up to 1,600 nits of peak brightness, which is amazing in all lighting situations. The screen also delivers 120Hz buttery smooth visuals, which is fantastic if you’re doing any type of video editing. Connectivity isn’t something you’ll have to worry about either, as the laptop is future-proofed with Thunderbolt 5, Wi-Fi 7, HDMI, MagSafe 3, and an SDXC slot so you can hook up extra displays, connect drives, and transfer files with ease.
At $2,699, this is still a premium purchase, not a casual upgrade. The M5 Pro is built for power users, so if you’ll mostly use the laptop for browsing, streaming movies, and sending emails, this is a bit too much power for your needs. But if your work actually pushes a machine hard, this is a smart pick, because $400 off the MacBook Pro is a great deal.

title: Anker Prime Foldable 3-in-1 review: Fast, foldable 25W Qi 2.2 charging
content:
Macworld



At a glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
25W iPhone charging
Apple Watch and AirPods fast-charge
Compact and lightweight
Non‑slip Watch stickers
45W charger in the box
Cons
Smaller travel chargers exist
Our Verdict 
This good-looking and super-fast wireless 3-in-1 charger is compact and lightweight enough for travel but works just as well as a sturdy desktop charging station with an adjustable stand.
Price When Reviewed
This value will show the geolocated pricing text for product undefined
Best Pricing Today


Price When Reviewed
$149.99
Retailer
				
Price
				
$119.99						
View Deal						
$149.99						
View Deal						
Anker
													
$149.99						
View Deal						
$149.99						
View Deal						
Product
				
Price
				

								
							
						
What is it? A fast and compact 3-in-1 magnetic wireless charger for iPhone, Apple Watch and AirPods.
Who is it for? Apple users who want the fastest wireless charging speeds in a travel-ready package.
Who shouldn’t buy it? Compact for travel, users looking for a larger fixed stand might find it too tiny on their desk, or love its small footprint. 
We’ve tested many of the best portable wireless iPhone chargers, and the move to the fastest 25W Qi 2.2 means there’s little difference between the speeds of wired and wireless charging.
The Anker Prime Foldable 3-in-1 stands out by combining fast 25W Qi 2.2 wireless charging, active AirCool cooling to sustain peak speeds, a sturdy foldable travel-friendly design, and an included 45W wall charger. While compact enough for travel, it isn’t the smallest foldable 3-in-1 charger you can buy. Anker’s 3-in-1 Prime Wireless Charging Station utilizes Anker’s MagGo and AirCool technologies in a lightweight, compact form.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




How portable is it for travel?
The palm-sized Anker Prime Wireless Charging Station folds into a compact, pocketable form that’s about as long and wide as a playing card and a little deeper than a full pack: 3.74 x 2.38 x 1.22 inches (95 x 60 x 31mm). It looks a bit like a power bank, but it’s not—to use it you must plug it into a wall charger—it ships with an Anker 45W power adapter and a long 5ft (1.5m) USB-C cable.
It weighs 7.9oz (224g), which is about the same as an iPhone Pro with a case on.
The iPhone-charging pad is mounted on a stand that can be vertically adjustable from 0-60 degrees.
The Watch module is nicely hidden and protected when the charger is folded. Pull the iPhone charging module into stand mode and the Watch charging pad is revealed. Click it and it springs out, where you can straighten it for use.
It comes with two handy non‑slip Watch stickers, which is a thoughtful touch. I test a lot of the best Apple Watch chargers and I can assure you that the Watch often sits or hangs at a disconcerting angle. These should stop that, if it bothers you.
Behind that is the Qi charging pad for an AirPods case.

				
					

				
			The charger is available in either regulation Anker gray or white.
Anker




How fast does it really charge?
iPhone 16 and later: 25W
iPhone 12-15: 15W
Apple Watch: 5W
AirPods: 5W
The Qi 2.2 standard in Anker’s MagGo means up to 25W for iPhones 16 and later, while earlier iPhones still charge at Qi2’s 15W.
Does the cooling fan actually make a difference?
It’s important to state that this is “up to” 25W as various environmental factors can negatively impact this maximum wireless charging speed. The chief troublemaker is temperature. Batteries don’t like getting too hot, and charging creates some heat during the process.
If the iPhone feels like it’s getting too hot, it will throttle the speed of or pause/stop charging altogether. What should be 25W might drop to 20W, 15W or 10W or, when too hot, 0W.
The standard way around this is active cooling by fans. Anker’s cooling tech is called AirCool. The fans keep the connected iPhone as cool as possible—Anker claims that heat build-up is reduced by up to 30%—to maintain a steady flow of power without the phone shutting off power when it gets too hot. In Anker testing, a drop of 9.4°F increased charging speed by 24%.

				
					

				
			Anker’s AirCool fan.
Simon Jary




One potential problem with fans is they can be noisy. Anker measured the Prime 3-in-1 Foldable as operating at just 19dB while actively dispersing heat through its built-in airflow system.
You can hear the reassuring fan in action, but you really do have to bend you head right next to it to listen in. We wouldn’t regard it as intrusive in any way.
The charger has a small LED ring that you can single-tap to check the current airflow cooling status: solid blue when Airflow cooling is on; solid white when Airflow cooling is off; and flashing orange if the connected adapter is incompatible or a metal object has been detected on the charging surface.
Double-tap the light ring to toggle the airflow cooling on or off. The light turns off automatically after 10 seconds so it’s not a constant distraction as a nightstand.
ESR’s cooling fans are called CryoBoost, and Belkin’s ChillBoost. Mophie’s StealthCharge technology takes a different approach by moving all the hot components away from the charging pad and into the stand’s base in its Mophie 3-in-1 and 4-in-1 StealthCharge Wireless Charge Stands—thus removing the need for fans in the first place.
Many Qi2.2 chargers don’t even try to cool down the charging module or iPhone, and we expect those to rarely hit a full 25W.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




Speed testing actively cooled 25W
Only iPhone 16 and later support charging as fast as 25W; earlier iPhone are limited to 15W. Anything earlier than the iPhone 12 lacks MagSafe and will be lucky to get 7.5W even with very careful placement on a flat Qi charging pad.
In our real-world tests—I don’t live in or have access to a temperature-controlled laboratory—25W wireless charging took an iPhone 16 Pro from 0-50% in just over 30 minutes, compared to around 45 minutes for 15W.
That difference doesn’t matter if you always charge overnight, but if you charge during the day, you’ll appreciate the speedier charging.
Batteries slow down charging as they reach being fully charged, which is why we measure to 50% for a raw-speed comparison.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




Watch and AirPods fast-charging
The Watch charger is rated at 5W. While we’ve seen Apple Watch chargers at 7.5W, Apple classes 5W as Watch fast charging.
There’s no such Apple rating for AirPods fast-charging, but you can certainly charge them much slower than with this charger’s 5W Qi pad but probably not any faster.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




Is it worth the price?
The Anker Prime Wireless Charging Station (3-in-1, MagGo, AirCool, Foldable) is priced at $149.99 / £129.99. It’s also available at Amazon and Amazon UK where we have witnessed decent discounts.
That’s about the same as Ugreen’s rival 25W MagFlow 3-in-1 Folding Wireless Charger, although there are cheaper 25W travel 3-in-1 chargers such as the $79.99 Kuxiu X40 that folds into a smaller 2.5 x 2.5 x 0.7 inch (64 x 64 x 18mm) form.
Choosing between these 3-in-1 chargers should come down to design preferences. While the Ugreen is again smaller at 4.7 x 2.4 x 0.8 inches (12 x 6 x 2cm), the Watch charges at the back next to the AirPods rather than being suspended as on the Anker.
Of them all, the Anker feels the most sturdy, which is maybe the compromise for its slightly larger form.
Should you buy the Anker Prime Foldable 3-in-1 Wireless Charging Station?
This good-looking and super-fast wireless 3-in-1 charger is compact and lightweight enough for travel but works just as well as a sturdy desktop charging station with an adjustable stand.
creator: Macworld

title: ClickLock malware makes Macs unusable until victims surrender their passwords
content:
Security firm Group-IB has uncovered ClickLock Stealer, a new Mac threat that hijacks normal system behavior to pressure victims into handing over passwords and access.

ClickLock Stealer
The firm said the campaign has reached at least 100 targets in 33 countries since May 2026, with more than half of the identified activity in Europe. Group-IB also found targets in North America, the Middle East and Africa.
ClickLock builds on ClickFix, a familiar scam that uses fake CAPTCHA or Cloudflare verification pages to convince people to paste commands into Terminal. ClickLock adds a coercive step when a victim cancels a password prompt or refuses access.
The attack begins only after the victim copies a command from a malicious page and runs it in Terminal. The script then downloads separate components designed to steal passwords, collect browser and cryptocurrency data, access the macOS Keychain and install a persistent backdoor.

title: Apple Likely Left Jony Ive Out of Its OpenAI Lawsuit on Purpose
content:
Bloomberg's Mark Gurman says Apple's decision not to name its former design chief Jony Ive in its trade-secret lawsuit against OpenAI was likely a deliberate one, shaped in part by the company's relationship with Laurene Powell Jobs.


Apple's roughly 40-page complaint refers only to unnamed "former Apple leaders" rather than calling out Ive, even though he leads design on OpenAI's hardware lineup through his firm LoveFrom, built on the back of OpenAI's $6.5 billion acquisition of his startup io Products. The allegations instead center on former iPhone executive Tang Tan, now OpenAI's chief hardware officer, whom Apple accuses of coaching recruits to bypass security measures and bring Apple hardware into interviews for "show-and-tell" sessions.

"Power On" newsletter, Gurman notes that Apple's decision likely reflects, at least in part, a genuine absence of evidence against Ive, who is reportedly not closely involved in OpenAI's day-to-day recruiting or engineering. He argues, however, that the relationship between Powell Jobs and Ive was also a significant factor. The two are described as close friends, with Ive having delivered a tribute at Steve Jobs' memorial service in 2011. Powell Jobs was an investor in io Products and remains a supporter of LoveFrom. According to Gurman, naming Ive in the complaint risked straining Apple's relationship with a figure who continues to carry considerable influence within the company.

Tim Cook and his successor John Ternus. He characterizes her placement as a visible show of support for Apple's leadership transition.

400 former Apple employees are now reported to work at OpenAI, whose first hardware product is expected to be a portable smart speaker aimed at competing with the iPhone in the long term.
Tags: Bloomberg, Jony Ive, Laurene Powell Jobs, Apple Lawsuits, Mark Gurman, OpenAI
This article, "Apple Likely Left Jony Ive Out of Its OpenAI Lawsuit on Purpose" first appeared on MacRumors.com

title: StarTech USB4 Dock review: Native dual displays without Thunderbolt
content:
Macworld


At a glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
Driverless dual-display support (M3 and later)
40Gbps USB4
100W PD to laptop
More affordable than Thunderbolt
Cons
Relatively few ports

Our Verdict 
The ultra-secure StarTech.com 208N will appeal to frugal IT teams deploying high-performance dual-display workstations across different platforms with its networking and simple driverless setup.

What is it? An entry-level docking station that effortlessly connects up to two external displays without the need to install extra software.
Who is it for? MacBook users who want to natively maximize their dock display options without spending big on Thunderbolt, and IT teams who manage offices with multiple fixed monitor setups.
Who shouldn’t buy it? Base MacBook M1/M2/Neo users who require DisplayLink docks to use more than one monitor.
Most of the docks we recommend to Mac users use Thunderbolt, which offers the fastest speeds and the broadest feature set. USB-C docks are usually much cheaper, making them an attractive alternative, but they come with compromises. They often offer slower data-transfer speeds – typically 10Gbps compared to Thunderbolt’s 40Gbps – and most can’t provide two native extended displays on a Mac.
Because macOS doesn’t support DisplayPort Multi-Stream Transport (MST), a basic non-Thunderbolt dock can only mirror a second external display rather than extend it. There are workarounds. DisplayLink docks can support multiple screens, but require drivers and come with their own limitations. Other docks use two upstream USB-C connections, but that ties up two of your MacBook’s valuable ports.
Why is this USB4 dock different?
StarTech.com has a new solution that affordably gets past the macOS limitation without the need for Thunderbolt or DisplayLink. The StarTech.com 208N uses USB4 to deliver 40Gbps bandwidth and native, driver-free dual extended displays on supported Macs without requiring Thunderbolt, DisplayLink or two upstream connections. The result is a much more affordable dock that overcomes one of the biggest limitations of USB-C docking on the Mac.
This is particularly useful for IT teams deploying docks for offices with mixed platforms such as Windows and Chrome as well as Macs. It also keeps the cost of the dock down as Thunderbolt docks almost always cost more due to their tighter Intel certification.
The pragmatically but long-windedly named StarTech.com Driverless Dual 4K USB4 Universal Docking Station (or just 208N-USB4-DOCK to its friends) delivers driverless dual-display support on MacBooks without the need for Thunderbolt, DisplayLink or two Mac connectors.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




What ports do you get?
One upstream USB4 port (40Gbps, 100W)
Two USB-C ports (10Gbps, 7.5W)
Two USB-A ports (10Gbps, 4.5W)
One USB-A port (480Mbps, 2.5W)
Two DisplayPort
Ethernet (2.5Gb)
134W power supply
The first spec to shout about is the upstream USB4 connection that mirrors Thunderbolt 3 or 4’s 40Gbps bandwidth. USB4 is not full Thunderbolt but is compatible with it and offers a more affordable high-bandwidth connection to the Mac. As this dock supports DisplayPort video it compares well to Thunderbolt. Only Thunderbolt 5 is faster.
The dock can deliver up to 100W of power to the connected laptop, which is enough to fast-charge all but the 16-inch MacBook Pro.
With only eight downstream ports, the StarTech.com 208N is a little shy compared to docks with ten or more ports to expand on the MacBook’s own paltry number.
There are two extra USB-C ports, both at 10Gbps and one of which can carry a DP Alt-Mode video signal to a USB-C monitor.
There are three legacy USB-A ports, which should be more than enough these days unless you still prefer wired keyboard and mouse. Two of these ports are rated at 10Gbps and one at the lowly USB 2.0 speed of 480Mbps.
With the exception of the 100W PD to the connected laptop, none of the other USB ports can output much in the way of charge, as suggested by the low 134W power supply. Most docks are equipped with 180W or higher power supplies to support better device charging from their USB ports. An iPhone requires at least 18W for fast-charging—7.5W will charge a phone but very slowly, although it’s fine if you just want to perk up your Apple Watch or AirPods.
StarTech.com is marketing this dock as an entry-level dock focused on the external screen support, but one 15W port at least would have been a bonus.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




Network support is fast, offering up to 2.5Gbps compared to the standard 1Gbps Ethernet. This is backwards compatible to Gigabit Ethernet networks and will work with even 10Gbps networks although at a maximum 2.5Gbps. 
The upstream USB4 port is securely screwed into place to prevent accidental disconnections. The dock also includes integrated lock slots and VESA or surface mounting options to deter theft and tampering. These are all ticked boxes for IT office staff.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




How does it avoid DisplayLink drivers?
Thunderbolt docks can handle as many displays as they have ports and your connected Mac supports.
USB-C docks are hampered by their lower bandwidth and the macOS lack of Multi-Stream Transport that is no brake for Windows PCs. This limits them to the second connected external screen only mirroring what is the first. The usual solution—save buying a more expensive Thunderbolt dock—is to get a dock that supports DisplayLink software that can push past the limitation through compression.
Aside from the compression and potential latency itself, DisplayLink docks won’t work with most streaming services—AppleTV, Disney+, Netflix etc—due to DRM-protected content restrictions.
The StarTech.com 208N gets past this limitation and matches Thunderbolt 4 on 40Gbps bandwidth, and is able to provide a lag-free second external display with a properly extended, non-mirrored screen image by using native USB4 and GPU-based video output.
The technical stuff: It uses Intel’s Hoover Ridge USB4 controller, which supports native DisplayPort video transport over USB4 using DisplayPort tunneling. Rather than generating video in software (as DisplayLink-based docks do), the dock carries the native DisplayPort streams produced by the host GPU and routes them directly to its video outputs. As a result, the operating system detects the connected monitors as native displays, without requiring any additional graphics drivers or software.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




You can choose from the two DisplayPort dedicated video ports or the video-enabled USB-C port at the back of the dock. If you are using the USB-C port for video, it won’t support other data at the same time.
Resolution, as with all docks, is dependent on the capability of your connected Mac. 
Base M1/M2 Macs and MacBook Neo support just a single display, so this dock is less relevant
M1/M2/M3 Pro Macs support a single 8K/60Hz or 4K/144Hz monitor or two 6K/60Hz
M4/M5 Pro and Max, plus base M5 Macs support up to the same 8K/60Hz single or faster-refresh 4K/240Hz single, plus dual 6K/60Hz or dual 4K/144Hz

				
					

				
			
StarTech.com




Price
The StarTech.com Driverless Dual 4K USB4 Universal Docking Station (208N-USB4-DOCK) is priced at $143.99 / £141.59, which is significantly cheaper than you’ll find in a Thunderbolt dock.
The dock is clever enough to skip most of the limitations found in other non-Thunderbolt docks for Mac. If you require Thunderbolt functions such as daisy-chaining or certified faster bandwidth, check out our reviews of the best Thunderbolt docks.
Should you buy the StarTech.com 208N?
We’d prefer better device charging from at least one of the five USB ports, but being more affordable than a Thunderbolt dock, the ultra-secure StarTech.com 208N will appeal to IT teams deploying high-performance dual-display workstations across different platforms with its networking and simple driverless setup.
creator: Macworld

title: macOS 27 has a secret popover menu for AI power users
content:
Macworld


When Apple unveiled the new Siri AI as part of the OS 27 updates coming this fall, it showed off a new interface, a new app, and a whole new personality for the revamped chatbot. But there’s a hidden feature Apple didn’t tell us about.
As spotted by Reddit user pdfu and posted in the macOS Beta subreddit, the latest macOS betas (developer beta 3 and public beta 1) contain a hidden “floating contextual interface” for the new Siri that can pop up while selecting text. Among the actions available to choose from are:
Writing Tools 
Create Key Points, Summarize
Add to Contacts, Message, Email
Create Event
Show in Maps
Track Flight, Track Package
The post includes a short video demonstrating the feature in action, and it’s pretty neat. Rather than Control-click to bring up the Apple Intelligence options, a small translucent Siri icon will appear next to any selected text. Hover over it, and a set of actions will appear.
It’s unclear why Apple hasn’t enabled this feature for all users, but presumably it’s because the feature is buggy enough to remain in testing. Or perhaps it’s an internal feature for Apple employees that isn’t meant for public use.
At any rate, you can try it out if you want through Apple’s FeatureFlags tool. We’ll caution that it uses the dreaded sudo command, which gives you the temporary ability to access system-level permissions. As long as you copy and paste the command properly, there shouldn’t be any issues, but it’s worth making sure your Mac is properly backed up first, just in case 
First, open the Terminal app and paste sudo mkdir -p /Library/Preferences/FeatureFlags/Domain && \ to enable FeatureFlags to run on your Mac. Then paste sudo defaults write /Library/Preferences/FeatureFlags/Domain/WritingTools LightweightUI_macOS -dict Enabled -bool true and reboot your Mac. This will turn on the popover menu.
If you want to turn it off, type sudo defaults write /Library/Preferences/FeatureFlags/Domain/WritingTools LightweightUI_macOS -dict Enabled -bool false into Terminal and restart.
creator: Macworld

title: Apple Shelved Two Mac Pro Models, Including an Intel-Based One
content:
Apple developed two Mac Pro models in recent years that never made it to market, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman.


In his latest "Power On" newsletter, Gurman revealed that Apple internally developed two unreleased Mac Pro projects codenamed J170 and J190 before ultimately discontinuing the machine altogether.

As for new Mac Pro hardware, there were two additional models developed by Apple that never saw the light of day: J170 and J190. The former was a new Intel Corp. Mac Pro (yes, even when Apple was making its own silicon) to address certain uses. The latter was an M3 Ultra version that was supposed to launch alongside the M3 Ultra Mac Studio in early 2025.


J170 was apparently a new Intel-based Mac Pro, which is notable because Apple had already completed its transition to Apple silicon by the time the project was in active development. Gurman did not specify what "certain uses" the Intel model was meant to address, but its existence suggests Apple explored keeping an x86 option alive for specific professional workflows even years into the Apple silicon era.

Mac Studio in early 2025, but Apple shelved it before release. It would have paired the same chip found in that ‌Mac Studio‌ with the Mac Pro's unique-to-the-lineup PCIe expansion slots.

discontinued the Mac Pro entirely this year, ending its 20-year run. The Mac Pro had been left on the aging M2 Ultra chip from 2023 while the ‌Mac Studio‌ continued to receive refreshes, and it was priced roughly double that of a similarly configured ‌Mac Studio‌ despite offering essentially the same performance.

Extreme" chip variants, processors with twice the CPU and GPU cores of Apple's Ultra chips, for a future Mac Pro before winding down those projects over cost and demand concerns.

title: 'ClickLock' Malware Coerces Mac Users Into Giving Up Passwords
content:
Security firm Group-IB has identified a new piece of macOS malware in the wild that pressures users into surrendering their passwords via a barrage of fake system prompts.


Dubbed "ClickLock Stealer," the malware needs no exploits and no elevated privileges to work. Instead, the attack depends on the victim pasting a command into Terminal and running it. The command then executes a script, and everything else follows.

Group-IB did not directly observe how victims are lured into pasting the command, but based on the script's behavior, the firm believes it's served through a fake "ClickFix" page posing as a Cloudflare check or browser verification step. Such pages instruct visitors to copy a command and run it in Terminal as a supposed "verification requirement."

Once it's up and running, the script discreetly downloads several modules and shows a terminal-based loading animation mimicking a Cloudflare progress bar with browser verification. If a user declines the initial password dialog that appears, the malware starts locking system usage.

Specifically, the malware kills every visible app every 210 milliseconds while the password prompt remains on screen, and the rest of the desktop stays unusable until the victim gives in. Meanwhile, another loop suppresses macOS security notifications for roughly six hours.

If the user gives up and enters their password, a second, genuine macOS prompt is subsequently forced to the front, asking them to allow access to a Keychain item. Granting it hands over Chrome's "Safe Storage" AES key, which is what the browser uses to encrypt saved passwords and cookies.

With the login password and Chrome's encryption obtained, ClickLock then harvests browser credentials, Keychain data, password manager vaults, and any cryptocurrency wallets, and then sends it all to a Telegram bot. It also installs a hidden backdoor disguised as an iCloud process to give it continual access.

Group-IB says the campaign has been active since May 2026 and has targeted at least 100 victims across 33 countries. More than half of the victims were in Europe. 

added a warning that appears when the user attempts to paste a command into Terminal from a website, chat, or message, and blocks the paste until the user reviews it. (Opera browser also recently added a similar feature.)

Tag: macOS Security
This article, "'ClickLock' Malware Coerces Mac Users Into Giving Up Passwords" first appeared on MacRumors.com

creator: Tim Hardwick

title: Upgrade your home office with this $40 set of rollerblade wheels (really)
content:
Macworld


Buy on Amazon
The Office Oasis Rollerblade Office Chair Wheels – $39.95
View Deal
The best upgrade I ever made to my home office wasn’t my Mac Studio or Studio Display XDR. It’s a set of rollerblade wheels. For my office chair. Really!
We all just sort of accept that our office chair wheels are the way they are. Those little double-wheel plastic or hard rubberized (if you’re lucky) casters that swivel and scratch and make noise. They don’t roll well over carpet, and there’s an entire commodity industry selling ugly sheets of thick plastic to put under your office chair so they don’t mess up your hard floors.
It doesn’t have to be that way. I don’t even remember where I saw it recommended, but several years ago someone pointed me to a company that takes rollerblade wheels and puts them on office chair casters. Proper bearings, sturdier construction, and smooth polyurethane that rolls easily over carpet and doesn’t scratch up your floor.
The brand is The Office Oasis (there are lots of cheap knockoffs, but these are the original), and it’s only about $40 for a set of 5 casters in a variety of colors. Installing them is trivial—office chair casters are designed to simply pull off and pop on without tools.
I took a chance and couldn’t believe the difference. My chair glided so easily that when I put it on a section of floor that was just a little bit uneven, it just started rolling on its own. They’re whisper-quiet, too. You don’t realize regular office chair wheels are noisy until you get some that aren’t. And now my chair doesn’t need a big plastic sheet to protect my tile floor.

 
 @macworld.com 
The best $40 office upgrade I ever made 
 ♬ original sound – Macworld – Macworld 
  




The wheels also made my office chair just a tiny bit higher—about half an inch—which is a nice little bonus for a tall guy like me that usually has my chair extended up as high as it will go.
This isn’t a sponsored post (though we do earn affiliate revenue if any purchase is made leading from our site). The Office Oasis has never been in contact with anyone here at all. I have used these wheels on my office chair for several years after purchasing them myself, and I just love them. It’s one of those things you use all day every day and forget how great they are until you sit in an office chair without them and instantly regret it.
Spending $40 on a product that has made my life just a tiny bit better all day every day for years is a no-brainer.

title: Apple's Secret Siri Interface Buried in macOS 27 Beta: How to Enable
content:
Apple appears to be testing a hidden popover interface for Siri AI in the macOS 27 Golden Gate beta that surfaces Writing Tools and contextual actions whenever the user selects some text.


Spotted by a member of Reddit's r/MacOSBeta community, the floating icon appears at the top-left of the selected text, expanding when the pointer hovers over it to a compact popover of the tools and actions.

Currently, the popover offers the tools Rewrite, Proofread, "How does this sound?" and "Edit with Siri," plus options for creating key points and summarizing text. The contextual actions are based on the content selected, including things like adding contact details, composing a message or email, creating a calendar event, showing a location in Maps, and tracking flights or packages.

The Siri popover is disabled by default in macOS 27 developer beta 3 and public beta 1. Beta testers found it can be switched on through a private feature flag, but the feature is clearly unfinished. Some of the actions don't respond, suggesting Apple is still building out the underlying functionality.

Adventurous beta users can try it with the following Terminal command, followed by a restart:
sudo mkdir -p /Library/Preferences/FeatureFlags/Domain && sudo defaults write /Library/Preferences/FeatureFlags/Domain/WritingTools LightweightUI_macOS -dict Enabled -bool true

And to disable the interface, use this command followed by a restart:
sudo defaults write /Library/Preferences/FeatureFlags/Domain/WritingTools LightweightUI_macOS -dict Enabled -bool false

Tag: Siri
This article, "Apple's Secret Siri Interface Buried in macOS 27 Beta: How to Enable" first appeared on MacRumors.com

creator: Tim Hardwick

title: The iPad mini is running out of reasons to exist
content:
Macworld


When it first launched, the iPad mini wasn’t just small. It was also cheap. This made its selling point extremely easy to understand, which is important in the tablet space. If you want a big screen and a fast processor, inexperienced customers were told, go for the full-size iPad 4. Otherwise, save your money and get a more portable mini.
Over the years, the mini’s appeal has become more complicated, and these days it’s more difficult to recommend. Not just because the product has been neglected by Apple, which hasn’t released an update since fall 2024 and hasn’t released a proper update since fall 2021. It’s mainly because there are now alternatives which surpass the mini in almost every way, including price.
The original iPad mini cost $329 in fall 2012, the equivalent of $477 in today’s money. Yet the 7th-gen iPad mini now starts at $599. Inflation has been bad, but not that bad, as demonstrated by the standard mid-size iPad decreasing from $499 in 2012 to $449 today. In essence, Apple has switched the places of its two cheapest iPads, turning the 8.3-inch mini into a mid-market device with mid-market features, while the basic 10.9-inch iPad has become the budget option.
This might not have been a problem if the iPad mini wasn’t crowded out by other choices. But despite being offered an ingenious roadmap for free, Apple has so far resisted the temptation to simplify the iPad range, making life incredibly hard for inexperienced customers, who make up a large proportion of the tablet market.
There are four iPad sub-brands, one of which is just “iPad” so you don’t know if someone is talking about the brand or the range as a whole. One of these sub-brands comes with or without special glass, two in multiple sizes, and all four in multiple colors and storage capacities. There are three separate 11-inch models and it isn’t obvious at a glance which is best for a user’s needs. And the different iPads all follow different irregular update cycles. It’s a mess.
In that context, the only thing the iPad mini has going for it is size. If you want to save money, you go for the standard iPad. If you want the very best specs and features you go for the Pro. If you want an elegant (and more recently updated) blend of value and power you go for the Air. If you find the omnipresent 11-inch form factor a little too bulky and are looking for something closer to a Kindle, however, then the mini is the only iPad that fits the bill. But what about the iPhone range?
At 8.3 inches, the iPad mini sits in an appealing sweet spot: stowable in a handbag or rucksack pocket, while still having a big enough screen to enjoy a movie. But even that’s in jeopardy. The last two generations of iPhone Pro Max have offered 6.9-inch displays, which are perfectly decent for movies (and are attached to vastly more capable all-round devices). More ominously still, the upcoming foldable iPhone Ultra is expected to have an unfolded screen measuring roughly 7.8 inches.
Once the Ultra lands, the iPad mini’s portability mandate will be completely blown. Its newer sibling/rival will have very nearly as much screen size, while offering the ability to fold down to a far more pocketable chassis. And, like the Pro Max phones, the ability to do far more. A customer with an iPad mini still needs a phone. A customer with an iPhone Ultra has much less need for a tablet, and no need at all for a tablet that measures 8.3 inches.
Of course, the iPhone Ultra will be more expensive, perhaps as much as triple the price of an iPad mini. It’s not like this is a direct one-to-one comparison, nor that many buyers will have narrowed down their options to the iPad mini or the iPhone Ultra. The issue is more that it’s just one more alternative that takes away the iPad mini’s final area of superiority. (Bear in mind too that Apple’s budget phones are bigger than in the past; the $599 iPhone 17e has a 6.1-inch screen, entirely respectable for TV and films.)
Once the Ultra launches, there will be clearly better options in every single respect, leaving the mini as the choice for an incredibly niche audience with a peculiar combination of needs. Those who want a small device that’s cheap, but not that cheap, with some slightly upgraded specs, but not that upgraded, and are willing to use it in conjunction with a separate phone. It’s like a Venn diagram where one of the segments has been almost totally wiped out.
The good news, for those like myself with a soft spot for the iPad mini concept, is that Apple is understood to be finally preparing an update. This, according to the source who broke the story, will be the “biggest overhaul to the iPad mini in half a decade,” including an upgrade from the longstanding IPS LCD to an OLED display. We don’t know much else about the refresh, but it will doubtlessly include a new processor and hopefully a landscape front camera. Maybe, maybe Apple will deign to redesign the mini in some eye-catching way.
Whatever happens, Apple can’t neglect the iPad mini any longer. Or the iPhone Ultra’s first achievement will be to finally kill off one of Apple’s own products.

				
					

				
			
Foundry

"Extreme" chips would have had twice as many CPU and GPU cores as Apple's "Ultra" chips.

"The idea was to offer twice as many graphics and main processing cores as that chip," he said. "In the past, I've called this proposed processor the 'Extreme' chip, which to me is the most sensible Apple marketing name for something above Ultra."

Apple decided not to release the chips due to concerns over higher production costs and limited demand for the Mac Pro, according to Gurman.

title: System freezes plagued my Mac for months. A simple fix finally solved it
content:
Macworld


Ever since I installed macOS Tahoe on my M3 Max MacBook, it hasn’t been right. I’m not talking about Liquid Glass or window corner radii, or even the Apps Panel. For the better part of a year, my MacBook simply hasn’t worked the way I expect it to.
Some days are better than others, but at any moment, my Mac will freeze, with my trackpad becoming unresponsive and a spinning beach ball replacing my cursor. It only lasts a few seconds, but on the worst days, it happens dozens of times an hour and renders the trackpad completely unresponsive, as if my MacBook were shut down. Each occurrence lasts no more than a few seconds, but it’s enough of an annoyance to severely cut down on my productivity.
As I’ve written before, I knew what the problem was from the beginning. Activity Monitor showed a consistent CPU spike in the “core spotlight” task, followed by an immediate spike in the accompanying kernel. A restart temporarily helped, but the problem cropped up again before too long.
At first I thought it was related to indexing, but it lasted far too long. Then I waited for updates—26.1, 26.2, and so on—but none fixed the problem for more than a couple of days. As I wrote in my last article about the issue, turning off some of the toggles in the Spotlight settings helped for a while, but after a month or so, the problems crept back in. And since there’s no way to disable Spotlight completely without some scary Terminal tinkering, I was stuck with a Mac that didn’t work right.
Throw it all away
For whatever reason, the issue has gotten progressively worse over the past couple of months. It appears to be related to the installation of macOS 26.5, but it could also be related to the Apple Creator Studio version of Pages, which exhibited its own issues.
How it works is that Apple builds an occasional Spotlight index, which is what it uses to access files as you search for them. The Pages issue seems to stem from its use of iCloud as a storage drive. (I’m not alone in suspecting this either; one of my Macworld colleagues experienced a similar issue with Spotlight and Pages.)

				
					

				
			
My problems got so bad that the Spotlight settings wouldn’t even load.

Foundry




Since there’s no way to fully turn off Spotlight as you can with Siri or Apple Intelligence, I tried a few things. First, I turned off the Pages toggle in the Spotlight settings. That didn’t work. Then I tried adding the Pages app and its Document folder to the Search Privacy option in Spotlight settings. No dice.
In a fit of exhaustion, I even added my entire MacBook drive to Search Privacy to prevent anything from being searched and again forced Spotlight to rebuild its index. That didn’t work either. Neither did macOS 25.5.1 or 25.5.2.
So I was stuck. My plan was to wait until macOS 26.6 arrived and see if that helped (though after five previous updates, I had little hope that it would), and perhaps give the macOS 27 public beta a shot. Otherwise, I would just deal with the freezes and hope they didn’t get any worse.
Unfortunately, they did. It got to the point one day where whenever I clicked on a new window, my MacBook would stall for a second or two before it let me do anything. I was ready to erase the whole thing and start over—but first I decided to root around my Library folder.
I don’t know why I didn’t think of doing this before. Basically, every app and feature has associated files that can be accessed if you find the right folder. I couldn’t find anything obvious, but a quick Google search told me where they were hiding: ~/Library/Metadata/CoreSpotlight. To find it, click on your desktop, then the Go menu at the top, then scroll down to Go to Folder, paste that whole string, and press Return.

				
					

				
			
Foundry




You’ll see a bunch of files and folders. Mine was filled with nearly 10,000 files and over 2GB of data. I had no idea where the problem lay or where to begin, and didn’t really care about breaking Spotlight, so I dragged the whole folder into the Trash and selected Delete Immediately. 
Two weeks later, there hasn’t been a single freeze, pause, or stutter on my MacBook. I’ve even flipped all of the Spotlight toggles back to green, and I still haven’t experienced any issues. For the first time since I installed Tahoe, I can finally use it without fear of freezing—just in time to install macOS 27. Here’s hoping Golden Gate goes a lot smoother.

title: EU Orders Google to Give Rival AI Apps the Same Android Access as Gemini
content:
The European Commission today formally ordered Google to give third-party AI services the same access to Android device features that Gemini has. Europe's Digital Markets Act requires software makers like Apple and Google to grant equal interoperability to third-party apps and services, and the EC is enforcing it.


DMA interoperability rules are why Siri AI won't be available in the European Union when iOS 27 launches. While Apple tried to work out an agreement with regulators before launching ‌Siri‌ AI in Europe, Google did the opposite. Instead of asking, Google just launched Gemini integration on Android and opted to deal with the consequences afterward. Google's strategy gives Android users in Europe full Gemini access while it works on compliance with the DMA.

The European Commission is giving Google a full year to implement changes that will meet the Digital Markets Act requirements, and that's before any legal appeals Google might make.

access 11 features, such as:

 	
AI services must be accessible through voice commands like "Hey Google" or through access points like the home button or another activation button.

 	
AI services must be able to complete actions in and across apps, including completing long-running tasks in the background.

 	
Google must allow AI services to access context from apps and device sensors so AI can offer proactive services and anticipate user needs.

 	
Google has to give AI apps sufficient hardware and software resources, including access to its on-device AI models to execute tasks.


Google is required to implement the majority of the European Commission's changes by August 1, 2027.

Shortly after announcing ‌Siri‌ AI, Apple said EU regulators would not accept any of its proposed solutions to introduce the feature, and refused to engage on options that "preserve privacy and security." Apple wanted to use a Trusted System Agent that would allow third-party virtual assistants to safely access the same device capabilities as ‌Siri‌ AI.

Apple said the Digital Markets Act would require it to give any AI system "nearly unlimited access to a user's device," along with the ability to act on that information autonomously.

The European Commission claims Apple was "unable to develop interoperability solutions that meet essential EU privacy and security standards" and instead asked for a blanket exemption from the interoperability requirements, which the EC did not grant.

response to the EC's mandate, Google said the requirements "risk undermining vital privacy and security guardrails for millions of Europeans." Google hasn't given information on its next steps, but it says it plans to "continue advocating for a balanced approach that protects privacy and security while supporting market goals."
Tags: Android, European Commission, European Union, Google
This article, "EU Orders Google to Give Rival AI Apps the Same Android Access as Gemini" first appeared on MacRumors.com

title: Google Search Now Connects to Instacart, Canva and YouTube Music
content:
Google is expanding AI Mode in Search, allowing users to connect apps to improve search results and "get more done."


Instacart, YouTube Music, and Canva can be connected, and Google explains how the integrations can be useful:

 	
Instacart - If you're using AI Mode to help you plan a grocery list, ingredients can be added right to an Instacart shopping cart for quick ordering.

 	
Canva - If you're creating a flyer, you can get template options from Canva.

 	
YouTube Music - You can use AI Mode to curate a playlist and save it to YouTube Music.


Instacart, Canva, and YouTube Music are the only apps that can be connected right now, but Google may add more in the future. Connected apps were previously available in Gemini, but the feature set is now being expanded to Search.

an app is connected, users are able to access new features when making a request in Google Search AI Mode. Connected apps can be used in Search both on the desktop and through the Google Search iOS app.

title: Roblox is Adding AI Game Creation to iPhone and iPad
content:
Online game platform Roblox today said it is adding a new Build tool that will let iPhone and iPad users create games using AI.


With the mobile-first Build option, Roblox users can write a text-based prompt and have AI turn it into a basic game. A single prompt will create a starting point that can be expanded with playtesting and further commands. The entire game-making process, from creation to uploading on the Roblox platform, can be done on a mobile device.

Right now, creating a game in Roblox requires a Mac or PC app, but Build will extend game creation to mobile users too. Roblox is also adding new AI creation tools to Roblox Studio, its desktop game creation software. Content created using the Build tool can be iterated on with Studio.

Roblox Build is set to roll out to the Roblox mobile app on July 28, beginning with a public alpha test for users in New Zealand. Build will roll out to additional regions in the coming months as Roblox improves the experience.

title: Report: Apple Sends Legal Letters to Dozens of OpenAI Employees
content:
Apple has reportedly sent legal letters to dozens of former Apple employees now working at OpenAI, telling them to preserve potentially relevant documents and communications as it continues to pursue its trade secret lawsuit against the AI company. 


The Financial Times ($) reports that Apple has targeted around 40 former employees with legal preservation letters, acting on its belief that the alleged misappropriation of confidential information may extend beyond the individuals named in its original complaint. 

lawsuit filed last week against OpenAI, in which the company alleges a coordinated effort to obtain confidential information relating to its hardware engineering and product development. 

Apple claims OpenAI recruited key engineers, including former Apple executives Tang Tan and Chang Liu, and benefited from proprietary designs, manufacturing processes, and other trade secrets. Tan is OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer and a 24-year Apple veteran who led product design, while Liu is on the hardware team at OpenAI after working as a senior system electrical engineer at Apple.

Bloomberg this week that it is "not aware of any evidence that this complaint has merit."
